prompt: portrait of extreme close-up of a software engineer in a dark server room , filling the frame, shallow depth of field, intimate detail, three-quarter view facing right, positioned in left third of frame, right side of frame empty and dark, warm light on face, cold blue fill from behind. a moment of impact frozen in time — something is about to break, fall, or vanish. The subject is caught in the last second before change. The environment is tech — screens, server lights, a wall of stacked monitors showing dense terminal outputs, but the human element is raw and physical. [Visual distortion: reflection-mismatch] THE CENTRAL VISUAL: the subject grips a thick bundle of severed cables in their fist, the cut ends clean and fresh, sparks still dying on the floor — they just disconnected something critical and their face shows they know it. This is the first thing the viewer sees. Everything else in the frame exists to support this detail.. The inconsistent element is in sharp focus at the center of the frame. The rest of the scene is slightly softer. The eye must hit the wrong detail first.. Visual tension: imminent loss — the viewer sees something about to disappear and feels urgency. Key visual detail: motion blur on one element while everything else is frozen, or cracks spreading through a solid surface. the amber light of server rack LEDs in a dark room or a printed stack of code pages with red pen annotations on skin or floating in the air around the subject. warm orange key light on subject, cold blue fill light, cinematic color contrast, extremely blurred background bokeh, out-of-focus city lights, very dark, shallow depth of field f/1.4, contemplative, tension, duality, thoughtful, cinematic portrait. editorial documentary photography, shot on 35mm film, available light only, no post-production color grading, slight grain. brushed aluminum, matte black plastic, dusty glass, cable rubber, industrial concrete floors. Shot like a documentary photograph of a real workplace. Physical objects have weight, dust, and wear. Technology is infrastructure — racks, cables, screens — not magic. Available light only. No visual effects that could not exist in a real photograph.. 4:5 aspect ratio, no text or writing in the image, single subject focus
